Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The red rectangle is now vertical (toggled from horizontal). The level is not completed. Let me check the current state and try moving the green square to different positions with this new toggle state.
Current state:
- Red rectangle: vertical
- Blue square: lower position
- Green square: bottom-right of gray box
Let me try moving the green square to different positions with this toggle state. But I've already used 29 actions, and I have about 30 actions left. I need to be more efficient.
Let me think about what the goal might be. The right panel has two shapes: red and blue. These shapes might represent the "target" configuration. The goal might be to arrange the left panel objects to match these shapes.
Looking at the right panel shapes:
- Red shape: a stepped pattern (wider at bottom)
- Blue shape: a stepped pattern (wider at bottom)
The red shape is wider at the bottom, which might correspond to the red rectangle being horizontal (wider). But I just toggled it to vertical, which is the opposite.
Wait, maybe the goal is to match the left panel objects to the right panel shapes in a different way. Let me look at the shapes more carefully.
The red shape on the right is at [17,21]x[42,54]. Looking at the boundary:
[[17, 45], [17, 51], [19, 51], [20, 52], [20, 54], [21, 54], [21, 42], [20, 42], [20, 44], [19, 45]]
This forms a shape that's:
- Rows 17-19: cols 45-51 (narrower top)
- Rows 20-21: cols 42-54 (wider bottom)
The blue shape on the right is at [34,38]x[42,54]. Looking at the boundary:
[[34, 45], [34, 51], [36, 51], [37, 52], [37, 54], [38, 54], [38, 42], [37, 42], [37, 44], [36, 45]]
This forms a similar shape:
- Rows 34-36: cols 45-51 (narrower top)
- Rows 37-38: cols 42-54 (wider bottom)
Both shapes have a narrower top and wider bottom. This might correspond to:
- Red rectangle: horizontal (wider) at the bottom
- Blue square: lower position (at the bottom)
But the current state has:
- Red rectangle: vertical (taller, not wider)
- Blue square: lower position
So the red rectangle should be horizontal to match the red shape. Let me toggle it back to horizontal and try different green square positions.
Actually, wait. I've already tried all 9 green square positions with red horizontal and blue lower, and none completed the level. So maybe the target configuration is different.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to match the left panel objects to the right panel shapes in a specific way:
- The red shape on the right is wider at the bottom, which might correspond to the red rectangle being horizontal (wider) and positioned lower.
- The blue shape on the right is wider at the bottom, which might correspond to the blue square being at the lower position.
But the red rectangle is always at the same position (rows 30-33), so "lower" doesn't apply. The red rectangle can be horizontal (wider) or vertical (taller).
Wait, maybe the goal is to match the left panel objects to the right panel shapes based on their orientation:
- Red shape: wider than tall (horizontal orientation) → red rectangle should be horizontal
- Blue shape: wider than tall (horizontal orientation) → blue square should be... but the blue square is always a square, so orientation doesn't apply.
Hmm, let me try a different approach. Let me toggle the blue shape and see if the level completes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the current state and toggle the blue shape
# Current: red vertical, blue lower, green at bottom-right
# Let me toggle blue to upper position
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 48}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
